chunk_summary: ARFID in adolescents requires phenotype-based triage and multidisciplinary management, addressing sensory, fear-based, or low-interest eating patterns with tailored interventions and medical monitoring.
learning_objectives: Understand the diagnostic criteria and presentation of ARFID in adolescents.
Learn phenotype-based triage strategies for ARFID management.
Identify medical instability thresholds requiring escalation of care.
Explore multidisciplinary approaches, including therapy and nutritional support.
key_facts: ARFID is characterized by restrictive intake causing weight loss, nutritional deficiency, or psychosocial impairment without body-image concerns.
Phenotype-based triage includes sensory-based avoidance, fear-based avoidance, and low-interest eating patterns.
Medical instability thresholds include bradycardia, hypotension, orthostatic pulse change >20 or BP drop >20/10, and electrolyte disturbances.
Targeted labs for narrow intake include iron, vitamin D, vitamin B12, and zinc.
Oral nutrition supplements can be used as a bridge but must be paired with food exposure strategies.
reference_quote: Avoidant/restrictive food intake disorder (ARFID) is diagnosed when restrictive intake causes weight loss, faltering growth, nutritional deficiency, dependence on supplements, or psychosocial impairment without body-image–driven fear. (PMC6494183)
